perturbation.original_text: Seller covenants and agrees that, unless otherwise required by law, from and after the Closing: (a) Seller shall cooperate with CompuCom at CompuCom's expense to protect and safeguard all of CompuCom's Confidential Information; and (b) Seller shall not, directly or indirectly, use, sell, license, publish, disclose, or otherwise transfer or make available to others any of CompuCom's Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller covenants and agrees that from and after the Closing: (a) Seller shall cooperate with CompuCom at CompuCom's expense to protect and safeguard all of CompuCom's Confidential Information; and (b) Seller shall not, directly or indirectly, use, sell, license, publish, disclose, or otherwise transfer or make available to others any of CompuCom's Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase "unless otherwise required by law" creates a potential conflict. If a court order or subpoena compels Seller to disclose information, this clause, as modified, could be interpreted as a breach of contract, even though Seller is legally obligated to comply with the law. This introduces legal risk because Delaware law respects legally compelled disclosures.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law recognizes the validity of legally compelled disclosures. By removing 'unless otherwise required by law', the contract now arguably prohibits actions that Delaware law permits or requires under legal processes, creating a contradiction and potential legal liability for the Seller if forced to comply with a subpoena.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 10 Delaware Code § 3904 (2023) - Suits and judgments by and against unincorporated associations. :: 2023 Delaware Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Delaware Code 2023 Delaware Code (here) 2022 Delaware Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Delaware Code Title 10 - Courts and Judicial Procedure Chapter 39. PLEADING AND PRACTICE § 3904. Suits and judgments by and against unincorporated associations. Universal Citation: 10 DE Code § 3904 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 3904. Suits and judgments by and against unincorporated associations. An unincorporated association of persons, including a partnership, using a common name may sue and be sued in such common name and a judgment recovered therein shall be a lien like other judgments, and may be executed upon by levy, seizure and sale of the personal and real estate of such association, and also that of the persons composing such association in the same manner with respect to them as if they had been made parties defendant by their individual names. Satisfaction thereof may also be obtained by attachment process. 13 Del. Laws, c. 32, § 1; Code 1915, § 4198; Code 1935, § 4676; 10 Del. C. 1953, § 3904; 59 Del. Laws, c. 104, § 2; Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Delaware may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The removal of "unless otherwise required by law" creates a contradiction because it could force the Seller to violate a legal obligation (e.g., a subpoena). The perturbed contract language now arguably prohibits actions that Delaware law permits or requires under legal processes, as explained in the Law Explanation. Although neither scraped snippet directly addresses compelled disclosure, the absence of the 'unless' clause means the seller is contractually obligated to not disclose the information, even if legally compelled to. This puts the seller in a position where they may be in breach of contract regardless of their actions.

perturbation.original_text: CompuCom covenants and agrees that unless otherwise required by law, from and after the Closing: (a) CompuCom shall cooperate with Seller at Seller's expense to protect and safeguard all of Seller's Confidential Information; (b) CompuCom shall not, directly or indirectly, use, sell, license, publish, disclose or otherwise transfer or make available to others any of Seller's Confidential Information; and (c) CompuCom shall not solicit or knowingly utilize any of Seller's Confidential Information regarding Seller's Services business from any former employee of Seller.
perturbation.changed_text: CompuCom covenants and agrees that from and after the Closing: (a) CompuCom shall cooperate with Seller at Seller's expense to protect and safeguard all of Seller's Confidential Information; (b) CompuCom shall not, directly or indirectly, use, sell, license, publish, disclose or otherwise transfer or make available to others any of Seller's Confidential Information; and (c) CompuCom shall not solicit or knowingly utilize any of Seller's Confidential Information regarding Seller's Services business from any former employee of Seller.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase "unless otherwise required by law" creates a potential conflict. If a court order or subpoena compels CompuCom to disclose information, this clause, as modified, could be interpreted as a breach of contract, even though CompuCom is legally obligated to comply with the law. This introduces legal risk because Delaware law respects legally compelled disclosures.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law recognizes the validity of legally compelled disclosures. By removing 'unless otherwise required by law', the contract now arguably prohibits actions that Delaware law permits or requires under legal processes, creating a contradiction and potential legal liability for CompuCom if forced to comply with a subpoena.

perturbation.justification: The original contract acknowledges exceptions to confidentiality obligations when 'required by law.' Removing this phrase creates a direct conflict because the modified contract now lacks any exception for legally compelled disclosures. While the provided law snippet (10 Delaware Code § 3904) does not directly address confidentiality or compelled disclosure, the perturbation explanation and law explanation both assert that Delaware law recognizes legally compelled disclosures. Therefore, the omission directly contradicts the assumed legal principle by creating an absolute obligation of confidentiality where none should exist, potentially placing CompuCom in a position of breaching the contract if forced to comply with a subpoena.

perturbation.original_text: The parties acknowledge that money damages are not an adequate remedy for violations of this Agreement and that any party may, in its sole discretion, apply to a court of competent jurisdiction for specific performance or injunctive or such other relief as such court may deem just and proper in order to enforce this Agreement or prevent any violation hereof and, to the extent permitted by applicable law, each party waives any objection to the imposition of such relief.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties acknowledge that money damages are not an adequate remedy for violations of this Agreement and that any party may, in its sole discretion, apply to a court of competent jurisdiction for specific performance or injunctive or such other relief as such court may deem just and proper in order to enforce this Agreement or prevent any violation hereof.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'to the extent permitted by applicable law, each party waives any objection to the imposition of such relief', we are removing a clause that explicitly respects legal constraints on the court's power to grant specific performance or injunctive relief. This can create legal issues because courts may be restricted in granting some kinds of relief that would violate Delaware's constitutional law or other statutory obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Delaware Constitution outlines the jurisdiction and powers of the Court of Chancery. By omitting the phrase "to the extent permitted by applicable law," the contract implies that the parties can waive objections to any form of relief, regardless of whether the court has the constitutional authority to grant it. This could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent the constitutional limitations on the court's power.
perturbation.justification: The original text includes a waiver of objection to relief only 'to the extent permitted by applicable law.' The changed text removes this qualifier. The Law Explanation states that the deletion implies parties can waive objections regardless of the court's constitutional authority, potentially circumventing constitutional limits on the court's power. Because the phrase limits what a party can waive, removing it contradicts the court's power to determine the applicability of a waiver.
